stories.story: Police in the Indian city of Malegaon, in the western state of Maharashtra, are requiring identity cards for an unusual group of residents: Cattle. Following a recent state-wide ban on the sale and consumption of beef in Maharashtra, authorities in Malegaon have asked residents to take a 'mugshot' of their cattle and submit it to the police. Along with the photograph, the residents have to give information about their animal's 'unique features,' such as the coloring and age of the cow, along with the length of its tail. Police officials in Malegaon believe this is the only way to solve cow slaughter cases and enforce the law in Maharashtra. Mahesh Sawai, Deputy Superintendent of Malegaon Police, told CNN that they are creating a database to quickly check if a cow is present at a resident's place if information of a cow slaughter is received.
key_facts: Malegaon police require cattle ID cards
Maharashtra beef ban prompted this
Residents submit mugshots and unique features
Police aim to solve cow slaughter cases
Mahesh Sawai (Deputy Superintendent of Malegaon Police) confirmed database creation
explicit_unknowns: What is the exact date the state-wide ban on beef was implemented?
What is the specific age of the cows required for identification?

stories.story: Mahesh Sawai, Deputy Superintendent of Malegaon Police, believes the cattle identity card initiative will be very effective in the Indian city of Malegaon, in the western state of Maharashtra. So far, over 100 owners in Malegaon have complied with the police order, and more are lining up outside police stations across the city to get their livestock photographed. The ruling by police in Malegaon came in the wake of a recent case of cow slaughter in Malegaon, where two men have been charged for killing the animal and selling its meat. The Maharashtra Animal Preservation Bill now includes bans on the killing of bulls and bullocks in its list of non-bailable offenses within Maharashtra. Even the consumption or sale of beef in Maharashtra could now land individuals in prison for five years.
key_facts: Mahesh Sawai (Deputy Superintendent of Malegaon Police) believes initiative is effective
Over 100 owners complied in Malegaon
Ruling followed a cow slaughter case in Malegaon
Two men charged for killing and selling meat
Maharashtra Animal Preservation Bill bans killing bulls/bullocks
Beef consumption/sale carries 5-year prison sentence in Maharashtra
explicit_unknowns: What are the names of the two men charged in Malegaon?
What is the specific date the Maharashtra Animal Preservation Bill was enacted?

stories.story: The Maharashtra Animal Preservation Bill, which bans the killing of bulls and bullocks and makes consumption or sale of beef punishable by five years in prison in Maharashtra, has drawn strong reactions. Beef traders in the Indian state of Maharashtra strongly reacted to the decision and called a month-long strike, which ended Wednesday. The beef traders refused to even slaughter buffaloes, despite the slaughter of buffaloes still being permissible in Maharashtra, to deprive the state of all bovine meat. These beef traders have now vowed to file a case in the state's high court against the ban in Maharashtra. Red meat lovers in Maharashtra weren't too delighted either, arguing the government doesn't have a right to interfere in an individual's personal preference.
key_facts: Maharashtra Animal Preservation Bill bans killing bulls/bullocks and beef consumption/sale (5 years prison)
Beef traders in Maharashtra called a month-long strike (ended Wednesday)
Traders refused to slaughter buffaloes
Traders vowed to file a case in state's high court
Red meat lovers in Maharashtra argued against government interference
explicit_unknowns: What is the specific date the month-long strike ended?
What is the name of the state's high court where the beef traders will file a case?

stories.story: The Indian state of Maharashtra is not the only state to tighten its laws on cow slaughter. Haryana state, another Indian state, has implemented a maximum punishment of 10 years in prison for cow slaughter, which is the toughest penalty in the country. Rajnath Singh, India's Home Minister, has promised that he would do all to devise a country-wide law against cow slaughter. This national push comes as the Maharashtra Animal Preservation Bill now includes bans on the killing of bulls and bullocks in its list of non-bailable offenses, with consumption or sale of beef in Maharashtra potentially leading to five years in prison. Cows are considered holy and revered by Maharashtra's majority Hindu population.
key_facts: Maharashtra is not alone in tightening cow slaughter laws
Haryana state has a 10-year prison penalty for cow slaughter
Rajnath Singh (India's Home Minister) promised a country-wide law
Maharashtra Animal Preservation Bill bans killing bulls/bullocks and beef consumption/sale
Cows are holy to Maharashtra's majority Hindu population
explicit_unknowns: What is the specific date Rajnath Singh (India's Home Minister) made this promise?
What are the specific details of the Haryana state law?
